name: walking-with-the-wind
description: John Lewis' deeply personal memoir of the Civil Rights Movement — from his childhood as a sharecropper's son in rural Alabama, through the Nashville sit-ins, Freedom Rides, SNCC leadership, the March on Washington, Bloody Sunday in Selma, and a lifetime of service in Congress.
useCase: Understanding the Civil Rights Movement from the inside, learning nonviolent protest philosophy, leadership under existential threat, the power of disciplined action, and the Beloved Community ideal.
knownRisks: Written from Lewis' perspective as a committed integrationist and nonviolent adherent — does not represent the more militant/separatist views that emerged later
Some names and events are recalled from memory decades later; minor historical discrepancies are possible
Explicit violence: beatings, bombings, lynchings, and police brutality are described in detail
Lewis' religious perspective (Christian nonviolence) shapes the entire narrative
